trialDescriptions.title: Wirksamkeit einer digitalen kognitiv-behavioralen Psychotherapie zur Behandlung von Insomnie (KVT-I)
trialDescriptions.summary: Ziel der Studie ist die Überprüfung einer unbegleiteten digitalen Therapie (www.somn.io) bei Ein- und/oder Durchschlafstörungen und Beeinträchtigungen der Tagesbefindlichkeit in einer breit angelegten, alltagsnahen Stichprobe, in welche sowohl Patient*innen eingeschlossen werden, bei denen die Schlafbeschwerden die alleinigen Beschwerden darstellen, als auch jene, die zusätzlich unter weiteren Beschwerden leiden. Diese Stichprobe wird mit einer Stichprobe verglichen, welche keine Behandlung erhält. Die Therapie basiert auf den Prinzipien kognitiver Verhaltenstherapie bei Insomnie (KVT-I). Überprüft werden die Auswirkungen der Behandlung auf die Schlafqualität, Depressionen, Angst, die Lebensqualität und den Einfluss auf das Traumerleben.
trialDescriptions.scientificSummary: Ziel der Studie ist die Überprüfung einer unbegleiteten digitalen Insomnietherapie (www.somn.io) in einer breit angelegten Stichprobe, in welcher sowohl Patient*innen, bei denen die Diagnose Insomie alleinige Diagnose ist, also auch jene, die komorbide unter weiteren Beschwerden leiden. Die Therapie basiert auf den Prinzipien kognitiver Verhaltenstherapie bei Insomnie (KVT-I) und ihre Effekte werden gegen eine Wartekontrollgruppe getestet. Effektmaße sind Veränderungen in der Schlafqualität, Depression, Angst, die Lebensqualität und der Einfluss auf das Traumerleben.

trialDescriptions.title: Effects of a digital cognitive-behavior therapy for insomnia (CBT-I)
trialDescriptions.summary: The aim is to test the effects of an unguided digital intervention for sleep onset, sleep maintenance problems, and daytime impairments in a broad real-life sample (www.somn.io) including both, patients whose sleep difficulties constitute their only problem and patients that also suffer from other complaints. The intervention is based on the principles of cognitive-behavior therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) and their effects will be compared with a control group that is set on a waiting list. We want to test the impact of the intervention on sleep quality, depression, anxiety, quality of life, and the impact on ones' dream experience.
trialDescriptions.scientificSummary: The aim is to test the effects of an unguided digital intervention for insomnia (www.somn.io) in a broad real-life sample including both, patients diagnosed with insomnia symptoms alone and patients diagnosed with other comorbid complaints. The therapy is based on principles of cognitive-behavior therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) and will be compared with a waiting list control group. Outcome measures are effects on sleep quality, depression, anxiety, quality of life, and the impact on the patients' dream experience.

observationalGroups.comment: Interventionsgruppe: Digitale kognitive Verhaltenstherapie der Insomnie KVT-I. Das Therapieprogramm somnio besteht aus 11 Modulen, welche unterschiedlich lang sind (ca. 20 Minuten im Schnitt, insgesamt ca. 3 ½ Stunden). Diese werden in einem Zeitraum von 6-8 Wochen durchlaufen.

Intervention group: Digital cognitive-behavior psychotherapy for insomnia (CBT-I). The therapy consists of 11 modules of different lengths (about 20 minutes per module, treatment time in total about 3 1/2 hours). These are completed over a period of 6-8 weeks.

characteristicDescriptions.primaryOutcome: - Insomnie: ISI Insomnia Severity Index (prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)
characteristicDescriptions.secondaryOutcome:
- Dysfunktionale Einstellungen: DBAS-16 Meinungen-zum-Schlaf-Fragebogen (prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)

- Müdigkeit: FSS Fatigue Severity Index (prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)

- Schläfrigkeit: ESS Epworth Sleepiness Scale (prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)

- Depression: ADS-K Allgemeine Depressionsskala, Kurzversion (prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)

- Angst: STAI-T State-Trait-Angstinventar, Traitversion (prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)

- Lebensqualität: WHOQOL_BREF WHO Quality of Life Questionnaire (prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)

- Wohlbefinden: WHO-5 WHO-5-Wohlbefindens-Index (prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)

- Traumerleben: MADRE Mannheim Dream Questionnaire; DTI Düsseldorfer Trauminventar (jeweils: prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)

- Albträume: Häufigkeit, NDQ Nightmare Distress Questionnaire; CAN Cognitive Appraisal of Nightmares (jeweils: prä, post, Follow-Up 6 Mon, Follow-Up 12 Mon)

characteristicDescriptions.primaryOutcome: - insomnia: ISI Insomnia Severity Index (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon)
characteristicDescriptions.secondaryOutcome:
- dysfunctional beliefs: DBAS-16 dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon)

- fatigue: FSS Fatigue Severity Index (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon)

- sleepiness: ESS Epworth Sleepiness Scale (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon)

- depression: CES-D Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale, short version (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon)

- anxiety: STAI-T State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon)

- quality of life: WHOQOL_BREF WHO Quality of Life Questionnaire, brief version (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon)

- well-being: WHO-5 WHO-Five Well-Being Index (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon)

- dreaming: MADRE Mannheim Dream Questionnaire; DTI Düsseldorf Dream Inventory (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon, respectively)

- nightmares: frequency, NDQ Nightmare Distress Questionnaire; CAN Cognitive Appraisal of Nightmares (pre, post, follow-up 6 mon, follow-up 12 mon, respectively)

trialResultsDescriptions.briefSummaryOfResultsDescription:
Study Objectives. Numerous studies worldwide have reported the beneficial effects of digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I). However, few focus on real-world study samples that reflect people in regular care. To test whether dCBT-I suits German regular care, we designed a randomized controlled trial recruiting a heterogeneous insomnia population.

Methods. Participants aged ≥18 who met the criteria for insomnia disorder were randomized to 8-weeks dCBT-I + care-as-usual (CAU) or they were set on a waitlist + CAU. The intervention group was followed up at 6 and 12 months. The primary outcome was self-reported insomnia severity, assessed with the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at 8 weeks post-randomization. A one-way ANCOVA with baseline score as a covariate was fitted to determine group differences. Secondary outcomes included measures of daytime functioning, quality of life, depression, anxiety, dreams, and nightmares.

Results. Of the N = 238 participants (67.6% female), age range of 19–81 years, n = 118 were randomized to dCBT-I and n = 120 to the control group. At posttreatment, dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in the ISI (Diffadj = –7.60) compared to WLC (d = –2.08). This clinical improvement was also reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were also observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, symptoms of depression and anxiety (ds = 0.26–1.02) and at long-term follow-up (intervention group only; ds = 0.18–1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency.

Conclusions. This study showed that dCBT-I reduces insomnia symptoms and improves daytime functioning in a heterogenous insomnia population in Germany with sustained long-term treatment effects in the intervention group. Our results underscore the potential of digital health applications, their suitability within regular care, and their role in facilitating the widespread implementation of CBT-I as a first-line treatment for insomnia.

Study Objectives. Numerous studies worldwide have reported the beneficial effects of digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I). However, few focus on real-world study samples that reflect people in regular care. To test whether dCBT-I suits German regular care, we designed a randomized controlled trial recruiting a heterogeneous insomnia population.

Methods. Participants aged ≥18 who met the criteria for insomnia disorder were randomized to 8-weeks dCBT-I + care-as-usual (CAU) or they were set on a waitlist + CAU. The intervention group was followed up at 6 and 12 months. The primary outcome was self-reported insomnia severity, assessed with the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at 8 weeks post-randomization. A one-way ANCOVA with baseline score as a covariate was fitted to determine group differences. Secondary outcomes included measures of daytime functioning, quality of life, depression, anxiety, dreams, and nightmares.

Results. Of the N = 238 participants (67.6% female), age range of 19–81 years, n = 118 were randomized to dCBT-I and n = 120 to the control group. At posttreatment, dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in the ISI (Diffadj = –7.60) compared to WLC (d = –2.08). This clinical improvement was also reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were also observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, symptoms of depression and anxiety (ds = 0.26–1.02) and at long-term follow-up (intervention group only; ds = 0.18–1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency.

Conclusions. This study showed that dCBT-I reduces insomnia symptoms and improves daytime functioning in a heterogenous insomnia population in Germany with sustained long-term treatment effects in the intervention group. Our results underscore the potential of digital health applications, their suitability within regular care, and their role in facilitating the widespread implementation of CBT-I as a first-line treatment for insomnia.

publications.description: Schuffelen, J., Maurer, L. F., Lorenz, N., Rötger, A., Pietrowsky, P., & Gieselmann, A. (2023). The clinical effects of digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia in a heterogenous study sample: results from a randomized controlled trial. Sleep, zsad184. https://doi.org/10.1093/sleep/zsad184

Rötger, A., Schuffelen, J., Maurer, L. F., Lorenz, N., Pollok, B., & Gieselmann, A. (2024). The clinical effect of digital cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia in sub-groups with depressive and anxiety symptoms: A secondary analysis of a randomized–controlled trial. Journal of Sleep Research, e14173. https://doi.org/10.1111/jsr.14173

recruitmentDescriptions.exclusionCriteria:
Übermäßiger Alkohol-/Drogenkonsum; Suizidalität;
Schizophrenie/akute Psychose; Epilepsie
